text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Public health AI risks include hallucination, bias, privacy exposure, automation bias, model drift, and performance loss on complex tasks.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Public health AI risks include hallucination, bias, privacy exposure, automation bias, model drift, and performance loss on complex tasks.
textPreview: Public health AI risks include hallucination, bias, privacy exposure, automation bias, model drift, and performance loss on complex tasks.

text: Responsible AI risk management is not a one-time checklist. Public health risks change as data, models, vendors, disease patterns, staff behavior, and community needs change. Agencies need repeatable risk review methods that can be used before.
textPreview: Responsible AI risk management is not a one-time checklist. Public health risks change as data, models, vendors, disease patterns, staff ...

text: Governance, documentation, validation, and monitoring are mandatory, not optional.
textPreview: Governance, documentation, validation, and monitoring are mandatory, not optional.

text:
Use this slide in the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 16, 14, 42, 17.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Governance, documentation, validation, and monitoring are mandatory, not optional.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 16, 14, 42, 17.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI risks in public health are not limited to technical errors. They include privacy exposure, hallucinated guidance, biased prioritization, automation bias, model drift, vendor opacity, accessibility barriers, environmental burden, and loss of public trust. Risk management should begin before tool selection and continue through deployment and monitoring.

Use this slide in the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 16, 14, 42, 17.
Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

Accuracy risk: the output is wrong, incomplete, out of date, or unsupported by evidence. Equity risk: the system performs worse for a subgroup, language community, rural area, disability group, Tribal community, or. Privacy and security risk:.
textPreview: Accuracy risk: the output is wrong, incomplete, out of date, or unsupported by evidence. Equity risk: the system performs worse for a sub...

text: Use risk tiers so higher-impact systems receive deeper review, documentation, validation, and monitoring. Require human review, source verification, and documented decision ownership. Test outputs with realistic public health examples before.
textPreview: Use risk tiers so higher-impact systems receive deeper review, documentation, validation, and monitoring. Require human review, source ve...

text: Escalate to governance when AI affects public communication, eligibility, enforcement, surveillance prioritization, resource. Pause or retire systems when risk controls fail, performance changes, or the use no longer aligns with agency.
textPreview: Escalate to governance when AI affects public communication, eligibility, enforcement, surveillance prioritization, resource. Pause or re...

text:
Use this slide in the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 16, 14, 42, 17.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Common Risk Categories, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text: Review subgroup performance, access barriers, language needs, and potential disparate impacts before a pilot expands.
textPreview: Review subgroup performance, access barriers, language needs, and potential disparate impacts before a pilot expands.

text: Test generated content against authoritative sources and require correction before public release.
textPreview: Test generated content against authoritative sources and require correction before public release.

text: Document unsafe, biased, private, or inaccurate outputs and route them through escalation, containment, and corrective action.
textPreview: Document unsafe, biased, private, or inaccurate outputs and route them through escalation, containment, and corrective action.

text:
Use this slide in the Risks, Limitations, and Guardrails training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 16, 14, 42, 17.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

What harms could occur if the AI output is wrong, biased, delayed, misunderstood, or over-trusted? What data, populations, or workflows are most vulnerable to error? What conditions should trigger pause, review, correction, or retirement?

text: Select a proposed AI use case and identify risks across accuracy, privacy, security, equity, transparency, workforce burden, legal authority, and public trust. Define one mitigation for each risk.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Risk register, Mitigation plan, Incident escalation criteria.
